text_original: The things which from of old have got the One (the Dao) are -
text_normalized: The things which from of old have got the One (the Dao) are -

text_original: Heaven which by it is bright and pure;
text_normalized: Heaven which by it is bright and pure;

text_original: Earth rendered thereby firm and sure;
text_normalized: Earth rendered thereby firm and sure;

text_original: Spirits with powers by it supplied;
text_normalized: Spirits with powers by it supplied;

text_original: Valleys kept full throughout their void
text_normalized: Valleys kept full throughout their void

text_original: All creatures which through it do live
text_normalized: All creatures which through it do live

text_original: Princes and kings who from it get
text_normalized: Princes and kings who from it get

text_original: The model which to all they give.
text_normalized: The model which to all they give.

text_original: All these are the results of the One (Dao).
text_normalized: All these are the results of the One (Dao).

text_original: If heaven were not thus pure, it soon would rend;
text_normalized: If heaven were not thus pure, it soon would rend;

text_original: If earth were not thus sure, 'twould break and bend;
text_normalized: If earth were not thus sure, 'twould break and bend;

text_original: Without these powers, the spirits soon would fail;
text_normalized: Without these powers, the spirits soon would fail;

text_original: If not so filled, the drought would parch each vale;
text_normalized: If not so filled, the drought would parch each vale;

text_original: Without that life, creatures would pass away;
text_normalized: Without that life, creatures would pass away;

text_original: Princes and kings, without that moral sway,
text_normalized: Princes and kings, without that moral sway,

text_original: However grand and high, would all decay.
text_normalized: However grand and high, would all decay.

text_original: Thus it is that dignity finds its (firm) root in its (previous) meanness, and what is lofty finds its stability in the lowness (from which it rises).
text_normalized: Thus it is that dignity finds its (firm) root in its (previous) meanness, and what is lofty finds its stability in the lowness (from which it rises).

text_original: Hence princes and kings call themselves 'Orphans,' 'Men of small virtue,' and as 'Carriages without a nave.' Is not this an acknowledgment that in their considering themselves mean they see the foundation of their dignity?
text_normalized: Hence princes and kings call themselves 'Orphans,' 'Men of small virtue,' and as 'Carriages without a nave.' Is not this an acknowledgment that in their considering themselves mean they see the foundation of their dignity?

text_original: So it is that in the enumeration of the different parts of a carriage we do not come on what makes it answer the ends of a carriage.
text_normalized: So it is that in the enumeration of the different parts of a carriage we do not come on what makes it answer the ends of a carriage.

text_original: They do not wish to show themselves elegant-looking as jade, but (prefer) to be coarse-looking as an (ordinary) stone.
text_normalized: They do not wish to show themselves elegant-looking as jade, but (prefer) to be coarse-looking as an (ordinary) stone.
